source.title: The hippocampus constructs narrative memories across distant events

confidence_rationale: Direct fMRI evidence that hippocampal patterns at event boundaries integrate distant events into coherent narratives. Establishes that boundary-driven hippocampal activity is not just a separator but an integrator of information across boundaries. Important for substrate design: boundaries are when cross-segment integration happens, not just when within-segment binding ends.
failure_signatures: Hippocampal activity at event boundaries was MORE similar between distant events forming a coherent narrative than between unrelated events. A substrate where boundaries only triggered RESET (not integration with prior narrative context) would lose this functionality.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal activity patterns at event boundaries are more similar between distant events forming a coherent narrative compared with unrelated events. Hippocampal pattern reinstatement at boundaries supports detailed recall of narrative-coherent events one day later.
mapping.ree_translation: Event boundaries are not just separators -- they are integration points where the hippocampal anchor system links the new segment to prior coherent context. The MECH-288 substrate should expose boundary events to MECH-269 anchor-set so the new segment can either start fresh OR be linked to existing anchor memory based on narrative coherence.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Cohn-Sheehy/Reagh stand in for the spec's 'Reagh & Ranganath 2018' citation -- Reagh is co-author and the paper is on event-boundary hippocampal mechanisms. The narrative-coherence framing is more specific than the original Reagh 2018 work but more directly evidences the integrative role of boundary HC activity.
mapping.source_context: fMRI of healthy human adults; encoding fictional stories with related vs unrelated events; one-day-delayed recall.

confidence_components.notes: Current Biology paper, strong methodology. Substitutes for spec's Reagh & Ranganath 2018; Reagh is co-author and the paper is more directly on the boundary-integration question.
